coredata.dc:title: Tris-assisted one-step fabrication of functional carbon dots for specific folate receptor positive-expressed cancer cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Lili
Wang, Xiuxiu
Zhang, Xue
Xu, Chang
Qiao, Meng
Chen, Zhenzhen
Tang, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Specific staining of cancer cells is momentous for cancer research. Nanoprobe with multivalent recognition is emerging as powerful tools for bioimaging, but the nonspecific cell uptake and complex functional modification procedures are still obstacles for specific detection and convenient synthesis. Carbon dots (CDs) with an intrinsic targeting ability, excellent optical properties and biocompatibility acquired from an efficient one-step fabrication procedure were urgently desired in specific cancer cells visualization. Herein, inspired by the interrelationships between interface and biomolecular mechanisms, we suggested that it was possible to construct CDs with the desired characteristics for folate receptor (FR) positive-expressed cancer cell imaging via rich hydroxyl groups Tris-assisted one-step hydrothermal treatment of folate acid (FA) and l-Arginine (L-Arg) precursors. The prepared small-sized F-CDs were equipped with abundant hydroxyl, pterin and negative charge surface, and possessed environmental friendliness, outstanding photostability and biocompatibility. Moreover, F-CDs had an intrinsic FR positive-expressed cancer cell targeting ability without any post-modification of the ligands. Rich hydroxyl groups play a vital role in endowing the optical properties and biological effects of F-CDs. F-CDs could be used as a promising candidate for FR-expressed cancer cell labeling and tracking. In addition, the caveolae-mediated endocytosis pathway of F-CDs was ascertained. More importantly, experimental results confirmed that the combination of physicochemical properties may provide an efficient strategy to overcome non-specific cell uptake interactions for cell labeling. Our strategy put forward a promising alternative to design fluorescent CDs for extensive chemical and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Folate receptor
Surface functionalization
Cancer cell imaging
Cellular uptake

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence imaging experiments in live cells
Cellular uptake pathway investigation experiments of F-CDs
Preparation and morphology characterization of F-CDs
Analysis of surface state on F-CDs
Investigation on optical properties and origin of F-CDs
Investigation on cancer cell imaging of F-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22134004 , 92253304 , 21927811 , 21705099 , 82073430 ), the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province ( 2018YFJH0502 ). National Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China ( ZR202102210262 ) and the Local Science and Technology Development Fund Guided by the Central Government of Shandong Province ( YDZX2022012 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The fluorescence results of products prepared from different hydrothermal raw materials. (b) TEM image of the F-CDs. (c) Statistic analyzed size distribution of F-CDs based on DLS result. (d) XRD pattern of F-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of F-CDs (red line), CDs without Tris (black line). (b) Full XPS spectrum of the F-CDs and the high-resolution XPS peaks of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, and (e) O 1s.

(a) The compared UV–vis spectra between stating materials and F-CDs. (b) The optimum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the F-CDs. The insert is digital camera images of F-CDs solution under nature light and 365 nm hand-held UV lamp illumination. (c) Fluorescence emission spectrum of the F-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (d) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of F-CDs. The lifetime decay curves of F-CDs, decay times and corresponding proportion, τ1 = 2.52 ns, B1 = 301.90 and τ2 = 7.93 ns, B2 = 349.56; average lifetime, τ = 6.76 ns. The average lifetime was calculated by a formulation: (B1τ1 2 +B2τ2 2)/(B1τ1+B2τ2).

(a) The photodynamic of F-CDs under a continuous excitation of 365 nm in 2 h. (b) Evolution of the fluorescence intensity of F-CDs in different pH. (c) Evolution of the fluorescence intensity of F-CDs in different ionic concentration. (d) Cytotoxicity evaluation of F-CDs with and without white-light irradiation.

(a) Confocal images in HeLa and HL-7702 cells at different incubation time point. (b) Fluorescence signal quantification corresponding to the above Hela cells and HL-7702 cells confocal image photos by Image J software. (c) Confocal images of HeLa, HCT-116 and SMMC-7721 cells stained 2 h via 10 μg/mL F-CDs.
Tris-assisted one-step fabrication of functional F-CDs for specific folate receptor positive-expressed cancer cell imaging.

head.ce:title: Tris-assisted one-step fabrication of functional carbon dots for specific folate receptor positive-expressed cancer cell imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong
Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Specific staining of cancer cells is momentous for cancer research. Nanoprobe with multivalent recognition is emerging as powerful tools for bioimaging, but the nonspecific cell uptake and complex functional modification procedures are still obstacles for specific detection and convenient synthesis. Carbon dots (CDs) with an intrinsic targeting ability, excellent optical properties and biocompatibility acquired from an efficient one-step fabrication procedure were urgently desired in specific cancer cells visualization. Herein, inspired by the interrelationships between interface and biomolecular mechanisms, we suggested that it was possible to construct CDs with the desired characteristics for folate receptor (FR) positive-expressed cancer cell imaging via rich hydroxyl groups Tris-assisted one-step hydrothermal treatment of folate acid (FA) and l-Arginine (L-Arg) precursors. The prepared small-sized F-CDs were equipped with abundant hydroxyl, pterin and negative charge surface, and possessed environmental friendliness, outstanding photostability and biocompatibility. Moreover, F-CDs had an intrinsic FR positive-expressed cancer cell targeting ability without any post-modification of the ligands. Rich hydroxyl groups play a vital role in endowing the optical properties and biological effects of F-CDs. F-CDs could be used as a promising candidate for FR-expressed cancer cell labeling and tracking. In addition, the caveolae-mediated endocytosis pathway of F-CDs was ascertained. More importantly, experimental results confirmed that the combination of physicochemical properties may provide an efficient strategy to overcome non-specific cell uptake interactions for cell labeling. Our strategy put forward a promising alternative to design fluorescent CDs for extensive chemical and biomedical applications.

Tris-assisted one-step fabrication of functional F-CDs for specific folate receptor positive-expressed cancer cell imaging.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Specific staining of cancer cells is an important goal in cancer research, as it enables direct investigation of cell biology, varied cellular processes, cancer diagnosis and therapeutics [1,2]. Fluorescence imaging with high temporal and spatial resolution appears to be one of the most indispensable platforms in a safe and cost-effective modality [3,4]. Intensive efforts have been directed towards development of fluorescent reagent that would target and selectively image cancer cells [5–13]. In particularly, fluorescent nanoparticles covalently linked with biorecognition ligands binding to receptor have shown great merits [6,14]. As compared to molecular dye-ligand conjugates, the multiple targeting ligands presented on nanoparticles surfaces allow multivalent recognition and binding to receptors on cell membrane, resulting in higher binding affinity and much improved selectivity toward targeted cells [15,16]. Versatile nanoparticles systems including semiconducting quantum dots (QDs), gold nanoparticles (NPs), upconversion NPs and dye encapsulated NPs, etc., have been developed [6,8,17–20]. In spite of great merits in targeted imaging, most fluorescent reagents have practical applications drawbacks in live cell imaging, such as the low solubility in water and toxic heavy metal composition of traditional quantum dots, low fluorescence quantum yield of gold NPs and upconversion NPs, oxidative biodegradation in biological systems of silica nanoparticles [21–24]. In addition, another notable challenge is complex functional modification procedures for the subsequent separation and purification in probe fabrication. Apparently, development of a simple, economic, eco-friendly, excellent photostability and biocompatibility, high fluorescence quantum yield and intrinsic self-targeting ability fluorescent nanoprobe with easy fabrication approach to manage these problems above is still urgent.

Owing to good biocompatibility, excellent photostability and tunable photoluminescence, fluorescent CDs have attracted significant attention and provide unprecedented opportunities in optical bioimaging [13,25–28]. With different fabrication approaches, the obtained CDs can be illustrated wide diversity in sizes, structures, surface function group and physicochemical properties [13,28,29]. Especially, the bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis provides a low toxicological impact of materials for biological applications [30]. In terms of cancer cells imaging, CDs exhibit homogeneous permeation and distribution for cells at early stage, endowing similar uptake accumulation for the normal and cancer cells [31–34]. This nonselective cellular uptake limits the applicability of CDs in targeting cancer cells research. Recently, several kinds of CDs are proved distinctive self-targeting by one-step fabrication approach, enabling their promising application in various fields. Zheng et al. designed the self-targeted CD-Asp to brain cancer glioma through a direct pyrolysis approach with d-glucose and l-aspartic acid based on the formation of RGD-like functional groups on the CDs' edge [35]. Li et al. developed self-targeted LAAM TC-CQDs with multiple paired α-carboxyl and amino groups that bind to the large neutral amino acid transporter 1 for tumor-specific imaging [36]. Zhang et al. revealed CDs with pterin and residual chemical groups from the FA thermal process still bond to the FR for cancer cells imaging [37–39]. These results indicated that the functional groups formed on the surface of CDs play a critical role in selective cellular uptake for specific staining cancer cells. What's more, it is evident that the choices of precursors and synthetic conditions have become powerful approaches to afford specific structures and properties for further applications.

Based on previous studies, size and surface charge of nanoparticles are also the most important parameters that control the cell uptake [40]. The CDs fluorescence mechanism show the surface/edge state and functional groups are critical for determining the CDs fluorescence [28]. Multiple information motivated us further exploring choices of precursors and synthetic conditions for self-targeted CDs fabrication and highly specific cancer cell imaging. FR which are rare for normal cells overexpress on the surface of cancer cells [27,41]. Due to the high binding affinity toward FA (Kd = 0.1–1 nmol L−1), FR can be used as a target to monitor cancer cells [13,27]. The hydrogen and hydrophobic bonds interactions between the C–N, N–CO, and N–C–N groups of pterin and amino acid residues of FR play a decisive role in the process of FA-FR binding [42,43]. FR positive-expressed cancer cell were selected as the model for the study on design and application of functional carbon dots.

Reportedly, nanocrystals could be endowed by surface-modification of dendron ligands for chemical and biomedical applications [44]. More importantly, M.P. Mathew and M. Xue discovered glycans and hydroxyl-rich hydrophilic peptide with no positive charge possessed the endocytosis-promoting properties [45,46]. This means and molecular recognition mechanisms of FR and hydroxyl-rich hydrophilic peptide inspired us exploring hydroxyl-rich functional CDs for highly specific FR positive-expressed cancer cell imaging. Here, we selected dendron-like tris hydroxymethyl amino methane (Tris) with abundant hydroxyl groups as an assistant to hydrothermal mix the nitrogen-rich precursors of FA and L-Arg for the fabrication of functional CDs by one-step hydrothermal procedure. The synthesis strategy and application in cancerous cells discernment were displayed in Scheme 1 . The experimental dates confirmed that the fabricated F-CDs owned small size, no positive charge surface, ample hydroxyl, pterin and residual chemical groups surface states. F-CDs displayed preeminent luminescence properties, high photostability, excellent biocompatibility and intrinsic FR positive-expressed cancer cell targeting ability. We demonstrated hydroxyl-rich Tris played the crucial role in the optical properties and biological effects of F-CDs. This study revealed that the combination of physicochemical properties (ie, receptor, surface charge, size) may provide an efficient strategy to overcome non-specific interactions for cell labeling and cell uptake. Our strategy provided a promising alternative to design fluorescent CDs chemical and broaden biomedical applications.

All reagents and instruments used in this work were introduced in the Supporting Information. The detailed synthesis conditional optimization experiment, stability analysis and cellular uptake pathway of F-CDs is listed in the Supporting Information.

The procedure for preparing the F-CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1. Briefly, FA (1 mg/mL, 1 mL) was mixed with H2O (1 mL), L-Arg (80 mM, 1 mL) and Tris (40 mM, 1 mL). Then the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon autoclave, which was heated at 200 °C for 2 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the crude product was purified by a dialysis membrane (MVCO: 500 Da) for 12 h. Ultimately, the powder was dried through lyophilization. The pure product was stored at 4 °C before used.

Dark toxicity and phototoxicity of F-CDs were evaluated by a standard methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromidestandard (MTT) assay. Typically, Hela cells with a density of 3 × 104 per well were seeded in two 96-well plates (control group and irradiation group) and cultivated for 12 h in DMEM medium (including 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics). Fresh DMEM medium containing F-CDs with different concentrations (0, 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 μg/mL) was added to replace the old medium. The two groups were incubated in a cultivator for 24 h. When incubated to 20 h, for the control group, the cells were incubated for another 4 h in the dark. For the irradiation group, the cells were cultivated with a white light (5 mW/cm2) for another 4 h. Then, the old DMEM medium was removed and washed with PBS buffer three times, then new medium with MTT (20 μL, 5 mg/mL) was added and reacted for 4 h. Finally, the medium was discarded, 150 μL of DMSO was added, and the absorbance at 490 nm was measured with a microplate reader. The cell viability was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging experiments in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: HL-7702 cells (human normal liver cells) and HeLa cells (human cervical cancer cell) were cultivated with fresh medium containing the F-CDs 10 μg/mL in 1 mL imaging dishes for 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, and 24 h. The cells were washed with 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) for three times. Fluorescent images were recorded by a confocal fluorescence microscope at different incubation time points. In order to explore universality of F-CDs for FR positive-expressed cancer cells, different FR expression levels cell consist of HCT-116 (colon cancer cell line), SMMC-7721 (human hepatoma cell line) and HeLa cells were seeded in three plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After incubation at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h, the cells were stained with 10 μg/mL F-CDs for 2 h. Then, fluorescent photos were also collected using a confocal microscope. The fluorescence quantitative data were obtained by Image J software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake pathway investigation experiments of F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells with a density of 1 × 104 cells per well were seeded in four same imaging plates. Four plates were marked number of 1, 2, 3, 4, and then incubated at a cultivator (37 °C, 5% CO2) for 24 h. After washing with PBS solution for three times, the cells (number of 1–3) were pretreated for 2 h with 5 μg/mL chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPZ), 10 μg/mL amiloride, 50 μg/mL genistein, respectively. The plate numbered 4 was cultured at 4 °C for 2 h in serum-free DMEM medium. After washing with PBS solution, fresh DMEM containing 10 μg/mL F-CDs were added in above four plates. Dishes number 1–3 was maintained at 37 °C for 2 h. The cells pretreated at 4 °C were incubated at 4 °C for another 2 h. Finally, the cells were washed with PBS for three times and cultivated with serum-free DMEM medium, fluorescent images were analyzed by a confocal fluorescence microscope.

Starting materials plays an important role in hydrothermal synthetic procedure for fabrication of CDs. Considering cross-linking and structural reorganization in hydrothermal procedure for functional surface state, we selected FA, L-Arg and Tris as raw materials based on FA and Arg with rich nitrogen and function groups and Tris abundant hydroxyl groups. As an important raw material, Tris could regulate the dissolution of FA and L-Arg. What's more, it may be a crucial factor for the photoluminence and biochemical nature of nanoparticles [44]. Based on the selected materials, different combined experiments of ingredients were first performed. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the fluorescence of CDs raised significant enhancement only when FA, L-Arg and Tris coexist, indicating the indispensability of Tris for the construction of excellent fluorescent CDs with FA and L-Arg.

Hydrothermal condition has an immense impact on the condensation, crosslinking, assemble and carbonization degree of starting materials. To gain strong emission F-CDs and insight into hydrothermal time and temperature factors during the F-CDs formation process, the products from different hydrothermal time and temperature were test. As shown in Fig. S1, the hydrothermal temperature and time is vitally important for the formation of high fluorescent CDs. Fluorescence intensity of as-prepared CDs is weak when hydrothermal temperature below 150 °C. Surprisingly, fluorescence intensity of F-CDs was sharply increased when the hydrothermal temperature raised to 200 °C and 220 °C. Considering the melting point of FA (250 °C) as well as functional group biological activity, we selected 200 °C as the following hydrothermal temperature. As the reaction time increased from 2 h to 12 h at the temperature of 200 °C, fluorescence performance of F-CDs was gradually decreased.

Based on above all date and results, we speculated the formation process of F-CDs as follows: at higher temperature, partial carbogenic CDs were formed because of carbonization. In 2 h, the resulted CDs would be carbonized and modified, and then generated stronger fluorescence, which was originated from the synergistic effect of carbogenic core and surface state. With the reaction time extended, the surface of the resulted CDs would be further carbonized and be destroyed, as a result, the luminescence mechanism of the surface state is also disrupted. The surface state plays a greater role in physicochemical properties of CDs during synthesis. Thus, in order to obtain CDs with high fluorescence and excellent surface state for cell imaging application, the hydrothermal condition of Tris-assisted the FA and L-Arg mixtures was maintained for 2 h at 200 °C for the fabrication of F-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and morphology characterization of F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: F-CDs was prepared via a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment process. To obtain more uniform-sized F-CDs, the as-prepared F-CDs solutions were first centrifuged to remove large aggregates, and further dialyzed in deionized water using a membrane for 12 h to get rid of unreacted small molecules. After purification, morphology and structure of the F-CDs were firstly investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fig. 1b image shows a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation. Sizes of F-CDs particles were around 1–4 nm in diameter. The hydrodynamic diameter was analyzed via dynamic light scattering (DLS). Corresponding particle size distribution (PSD) of the produced F-CDs was shown in Fig. 1c. F-CDs have PSD peak around 2.5 nm and are relatively uniform size. It is known that the cellular uptake process and cellular distribution could be influenced by particle size, shape, and surface chemistry. Our F-CDs was small size of 1–4 nm and no sign of agglomeration, which are important to make sure cell imaging application. The XRD patterns displayed a broad diffraction peak at around 2θ = 20.44° (Fig. 1d), indicating an amorphous structure [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of surface state on F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: XPS and FTIR investigation were conducted to analysis the element composition and chemical state of F-CDs. XPS analysis illuminated the different functional groups displayed upon the surface of F-CDs. Full-scale XPS spectrum represented that F-CDs primarily consist of three elements: C (69.66%), N (10.06%), O (20.28%) (Fig. 2 b). The chemical bounds shared by each element were characterized via high-resolution XPS spectrum. The deconvolution of C1s displayed three peaks at 284.7 eV, 287.8 eV, 285.8 eV, which were identified as C–C/CC, CO, and C–N/C–OH group (Fig. 2c). In the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), the three fitted peaks at 399.3, 400.1, and 401.1 eV can be assigned to CN–C, graphitic N, and surface NH2 groups of CDs, respectively. The O1s high-resolution spectra shown in Fig. 2e further confirmed the existence of CO (531.8 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (532.5 eV). FTIR spectra of F-CDs are presented in Fig. 2a. The FTIR results indicated that F-CDs (red line) displayed peak center at 2880 cm−1 and 1960 cm−1, which could be assigned to the amorphous carbon skeleton of F-CDs. The band range from 3600 to 3100 cm−1 and 3680-3200 cm−1 originated from stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, respectively. The stretching vibration of CN was positioned at 1376 cm−1. The characteristic peaks of CO, C–N, N–H existed in 1440 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, and 1200 cm−1, respectively. It confirmed the formation of amide bond during hydrothermal process. The spectra feature at 1484 cm−1 attested the existence of petrin, confirming that the petrin of FA. FTIR analysis similarly showed the presence of different carbon-bonded, nitrogen-bonded, and oxygen-bonded units arising from the synthesis pathway utilizing precursors reagent for F-CDs formation. These pterin and residual chemical groups on the surface of F-CDs ensure to bond with the FR.

To assess Tris participate surface-modification effect in hydrothermal synthesis procedure, FTIR were further surveyed in Fig. 2a, the peaks of O–H, CO and C–O–H from F-CDs were significantly enhanced at 3400-3200 cm−1, 1100 cm−1,1700 cm−1, 1250 and 1450-1300 cm−1 in comparison to the products without Tris-assisted. This result suggests that a large amount of –OH from Tris was successfully introduced to F-CDs. These surface functional groups determined the surface state, and affected its chemical and biological effects. As critical paramater of surface state, surface charge also plays an important role in administrating biochemical performance. From this perspective, the Zeta potential of F-CDs was investigated at neutral pH (Fig. S2). As compared to the products without Tris-assisted, F-CDs own a lower negatively charge forms on its surface with a result of −19.63 ± 0.69 mV. We speculated this result originated from abundant hydroxyl inserting the surface of F-CDs. As compared to positively charged NPs, neutrally or negatively charged F-CDs should have better biocompatibility and lower protein surface adsorption as well as antigenic. These advantages combined receptor-ligand effect may provide an efficient strategy to overcome non-specific interactions for cell labeling and cell uptake.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on optical properties and origin of F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra were analyzed for the exploration of optical properties. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the F-CDs features a strong peak around 288 nm and the shoulder peak at approximately 320–400 nm in Fig. 3 a, corresponding to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbons within the CDs core and n→π* transition of heteroatom doping. The broad UV–vis absorb peak in 320–400 nm was also echoed the high ratio of heteroatoms in F-CDs. To understand the effect of the precursor, absorption spectra of raw materials were further researched. FA features two absorption bands in which the peak at 288 nm resulted from π→π* transition of para-aminobenzoic acid and the absorption band at approximately 360 nm is assigned to n→π* transition of pertrin. Compared to absorption spectrum of FA, F-CDs shared the similar peak shaped with FA, whereas the absorption band moved towards the shorter wavelength, which indicated that pterin and residual chemical groups from the FA were successfully functionalized on the surface of F-CDs during hydrothermal process, corresponding well with the XPS and FTIR results.

Excellent fluorescence properties are the basis of practical application. Yellowish brown aqueous solution of F-CDs under nature light emits strong blue emission exposed to a UV lamp of 365 nm (Fig. 3b, insert). Fluorescence spectrum of F-CDs displayed the maximum excitation wavelength located at 365 nm with an emission center at 455 nm (Fig. 3b). Unlike CDs reported on FA or L-Arg by hydrothermal process, the emission of the F-CDs was dependent on the excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 3c, the emission center shift from 360 to 500 nm varied with excitation wavelength from 280 to 420 nm, along with significant variation of the fluorescence intensity. Also, when strong absorption 280 nm as excitation wavelength, F-CDs can hardly produce observed fluorescence signal. Such excitation-dependent emission behavior is understandable in terms of multiple photoluminescence center induced by the complicated surface state structures and the size distribution of F-CDs. Excited electrons return to the ground state via different radiative routes.

The absolute fluorescent quantum yields (QYs) of F-CDs was carried out by an integrated sphere. As shown in Fig. S3, at 365 nm excitation, the QYs of the F-CDs was measured at 10.75%. The fluorescence lifetime of F-CDs achieved by the time correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) assay was 6.76 ns on average. (Fig. 3d.). For the purpose of profoundly comprehending the fluorescence enhancement effect of Tris-assisted, the QYs and lifetime of as-prepared CDs without Tris-assisted was further measured with results of 5.44% and 6.81 ns, respectively (Fig. S4, Fig. S5). Comparative analysis shows that radiative transition rate constant (Kr, calculation by the equation: Kr = φ/τ, φ: quantum yield. τ: decay time, KrF-CDs = 1.59%, KrCDs = 0.80%) of F-CDs increased about two times, and the high ratio hydroxyl probably suppress nonradiative transitions with plentiful amino groups. In other words, the synergy effect of amino groups and hydroxyl can significantly optimize the ratio of the radiative transition rate and eventually contribute to the high QYs of F-CDs.

Photostability of F-CDs determined exploitable application depth. As the crucial characteristic, photostability of F-CDs under various conditions were evaluated. Fig. 4 a presented that no obvious photobleaching of the F-CDs exposed to 365 nm excitation for 2 h, indicating the excellent UV resistant of F-CDs, which allowed F-CDs to maintain stable fluorescence in practical application and distinct advantage for long-term cell visualization. The protonation and deprotonation effect of pH affect the surface state controlling the fluorescence performance of F-CDs. It was surprised that F-CDs exhibited a weeny fluorescence fluctuation in the pH variation range from 3 to 8 (Fig. 4b), however, the fluorescence of CDs without Tris fluctuated greatly in the pH range from 3 to 8 (Fig. S6). We supposed that chemical groups on the surface of F-CDs protected nanomaterials from the interference of pH. What's more, the F-CDs displayed excellent photostability in diverse ionic strength environment (Fig. 4c). In addition, the physiological stability of F-CDs in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and cell culture medium (DMEM) were also performed. The influence of medium on the fluorescence properties of F-CDs was negligible (Fig. S7). As a good performance of label reagent, long-term stability is pivotal for the promotion of market application. F-CDs preserved at 4 °C for half a year even maintained comparable photoluminescence to the newly prepared F-CDs (Fig. S8). Multifarious experiments manifested that F-CDs possessed outstanding fluorescence stability, which obtained broader opportunities in biological imaging applications.

In the past few years, CDs was high-profile for its preeminent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity superior to other fluorescent dyes. To assess the effect of F-CDs upon cell viability, the dark toxicity and phototoxicity of the F-CDs were conducted for the evaluation via a standard MTT assay towards HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 4d, F-CDs displayed inapparent toxicity even in concentration as high as 1 mg/mL regardless of precedence or absence of irradiation, validating the excellent biocompatibility and biosafety. We speculated that outstanding passive surface states make a significant contribution to the negligible dark toxicity and phototoxicity. Especially, abundant hydroxy functional group tended to form a corn-like structure effectively suppressing the diffusion of oxygen and reducing the generation of reactive oxygen species and other radical species under light irradiation. This negligible phototoxicity also keep with the above mentioned relatively highly quantum yield of F-CDs from Tris-assisted process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on cancer cell imaging of F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent photostability and biocompatibility and passivated surface states made F-CDs as a promising candidate for cancer cell labeling and tracking. We selected HeLa cells (human cervical cancer cell) with FR overexpression and HL-7702 cells (human normal liver cells) for the investigation on specific staining and long-term targeting imaging ability of F-CDs. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (Fig. 5 a) confirmed that F-CDs can be rapidly transported to HeLa cells within a short time of 30 min and located in HeLa cells for 24 h without notable fluorescence decay. On the contrary, HL-7702 cells were scarcely fluorescent labeled following incubation with F-CDs even for 24 h. Meanwhile, fluorescence intensity signal values were acquired in HeLa and HL-7702 cells via a soft named image J. As shown in Fig. 5b, comparative results indicated F-CDs own specific staining ability for FR positive-expressed HeLa cells. In addition, HL-7702 cells without F-CDs as a control group was also completed data acquisition (Fig. S9), the data demonstrated that nonselective cellular uptake was almost completely restricted. In order to explore universality of F-CDs for labeling cancer cell, various cancer cell lines with different FR expression levels were investigated, including HeLa cells (higher expression of FR), HCT-116 cells and SMMC-7721 cells (human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell and human lung cancer cell, fewer expression of FR). Confocal fluorescence images displayed that the fluorescence intensities of three cell lines were different after incubated with F-CDs under the same conditions, HeLa cells were the brightest, SMMC-7721 cells and HCT-116 cells were weaker (Fig. 5c, Fig. S10). The stain effect of F-CDs on cancer cells consistent with the expression levels of FR, which indicated that the prepared F-CDs could be used as fluorescent nanoprobe to for FR positive-expressed cancer cells labeling.

Based on the efficient targeting cell uptake and long-term cell retention capacity of F-CDs, we further estimated the endocytosis pathway of F-CDs. Different potent biochemical inhibitors consist of amiloride, CPZ, genistein were used to incubate HeLa cells for the inhibition of macropinocytosis-, clathrin-, caveolae-dependent pathways, respectively [40]. In addition, low temperature incubation aimed at restraining ATP-dependent transportation process through decreased the activity of ATP enzymes at 4 °C. Confocal imaging results (Fig. S11) signified that genistein remarkably reduced the internalization of F-CDs, ascertaining that caveolae-mediated endocytosis pathway for F-CDs. The combination of these two results reveals that F-CDs were transported to cancer cells via a caveolae-dependent endocytosis pathway, mediated by the surface FR receptor. The intrinsic cancer cell targeting ability and cell uptake pathway of F-CDs without any post-modification of ligands should be attributed to pterin and residual chemical groups, rich-hydroxyl, no positive charge on the small size F-CDs surface from the thermal process. We speculated the reasons are as follows:(1) pterin and residual chemical groups may bind to the FR through the hydrogen and hydrophobic bonds; (2) negatively charge surface of F-CDs exhibited lower affinity to the negatively charge cell membrane, and then overcome non-specific interactions; (3) rich hydroxyl groups promoted endocytosis.

To summarized, we introduced rich hydroxyl groups Tris to mediate the hydrothermal process of FA and L-Arg, and fabricated of hydroxyl-rich carbon dots with no positive charge for FR positive-expressed cancer cells labeling. Experiment data illustrated: (1) F-CDs own excellent luminescence properties, highly photostable, biocompatibility and intrinsic cancer cell targeting ability; (2) No positive charge surface, pterin and residual chemical groups provide reliable assurance for specific staining of cancer cell; (3) Rich hydroxyl groups play a critical role in the optical properties and biological effects of F-CDs. (4) Cell fluorescence imaging experiments proved that F-CDs possess good universality for FR positive-expressed cancer cell labeling. Our study reveals that the combination of physicochemical properties (ie, receptor, surface charge, size) comeback to their interrelationships in the bio-interface, which may provide an efficient strategy to overcome non-specific interactions for cell labeling and cell uptake.

Lili Tong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiuxiu Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xue Zhang: Validation, Methodology. Chang Xu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Meng Qiao: Methodology, Investigation. Zhenzhen Chen: Resources, Methodology. Bo Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22134004, 92253304, 21927811, 21705099, 82073430), the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province, China (2018YFJH0502). National Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR202102210262) and the Local Science and Technology Development Fund Guided by the Central Government of Shandong Province, China (YDZX2022012).
